topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and insistent, right in the center of my screen. My stomach did a sudden, unpleasant flip. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched. I quickly minimized the application, my fingers trembling slightly as I reached for the mouse. I knew, with a sinking certainty, that he had found my profile. Every carefully curated post, every slightly awkward selfie from the weekend, was now visible to him. I stared at the screen, the glow illuminating the sudden flush creeping up my neck. I felt my cheeks heat up, a burning sensation that made me want to physically disappear into my desk. The silence of my room amplified the frantic thumping in my chest.

[story 2]

I was scrolling through my feed, mindlessly flicking through pictures of friends and campus events, when I saw it. A picture of me, taken during the school debate competition, with a caption that was slightly different from the one I’d posted. It was Mr. Harrison. My heart started hammering against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat I couldn't silence. I immediately slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet library. I leaned back in my chair, trying to force my breathing to slow down. My hands were clenched into tight fists on the table, and I kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting him to walk in. The air felt thick and suffocating.

[story 3]

It happened during a late-night study session. I was reviewing notes, trying to focus on calculus, but my attention kept drifting to my phone. A notification blinked—a like on one of my posts. Then another. It was Mr. Harrison. A wave of heat washed over me, making my skin prickle. I instinctively pulled the phone closer to my chest, wishing the screen could just vanish. I felt a distinct tightening in my throat, a desperate urge to hide. I glanced up, hoping he wasn't looking, but the hallway was empty. I quickly locked the screen, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, as if I were trying to erase the evidence of my momentary lapse in judgment.
